summary: John and Jordan’s Valentines morning starts with a lot of glitter and ends with a lot of sex.
body:
Jordan wakes up to glitter on the pillow. 

It’s in his eyelashes, in the corner of his mouth, smeared over his cheekbone. His first thought is to panic, thinking one of the lads has played some horrific prank and dyed his face on a night out.

But then he hears humming. Soft, tuneless humming from somewhere near the end of the bed. Then it hits him – home. He’s home, at his and John’s bedroom, and there’s no hangover pounding behind his eyes. Just this… weird glitter.

He squints blearily down the bed.

John’s on his knees at the foot of it, wearing one of Jordan’s old Everton training tops that hangs loose off one shoulder but is somehow too short on him, and the tiniest pair of Valentine’s boxers known to mankind. Pink, with little hearts and arrows. His hair’s a total mess, sticking up all over, and he’s currently wrapping the footboard with fairy lights.

“What,” John says hoarsely, “in the name of fuck have you done to my bed, pet?”

John startles, nearly drops the fairy lights, then beams up at him like the sun’s come out. 

“You’re awake!” He clambers up onto the mattress, and crawls towards Jordan on all fours. “Don’t freak out, yeah, just stay there a sec.”

“I’d love to stay here,” Jordan mutters, dragging a hand down his face. Glitter transfers to his fingers. “If I can find my eyes under all this bloody–whatever this is.”

John snorts and reaches out, swiping at Jordan’s cheek. “Sorry. Might’ve got a bit enthusiastic with the confetti.”

Jordan blinks properly, lets his vision focus, and finally gets the full picture. 

There are paper hearts stuck to the headboard. Fairy lights, all around the foot of the bed. A little vase with three supermarket roses on the bedside table, and a tray on the floor with what looks like burnt toast, orange juice, and something that might have been an egg.

“Happy Valentine’s,” John says, suddenly shy. “Didn’t wake you, did I?”

Jordan takes a look at him. His cheeks are flushed, glitter on his collarbone. The big lad who crunches into tackles for fun is kneeling on his bed in stupid boxers, waiting for Jordan’s verdict like he’s about to be graded.

“Nah,” Jordan says, his throat going tight. “Didn’t wake me.”

He reaches out, hooks a hand round the back of John’s neck and drags him in. John squeaks, almost knees Jordan in the thigh but then collapses onto him, half laughing. 

“You’re all covered in glue, I swear,” Jordan grumbles into his hair, kissing his temple, then his jaw. “And you burned the toast.”

“Don’t be ungrateful,” John mumbles back, but he’s grinning wide. “There’s loads of jam. It hides the crispy burnt bits.”

He wriggles off Jordan to grab the tray, and Jordan props himself up on his elbows, watching him, that stupid ache in his chest getting worse.

He’s had flashy Valentine’s before, with other people. It’s always fancy restaurants and expensive jewellery, tickets to places he never really wanted to go to. This is… not that. This is glitter stuck in his boyfriend’s beard, and toast hard enough to break a window, and fairy lights all over his bed. 

He loves it more than anything he’s ever loved.

John sets the tray between them carefully. “Here you go. Michelin-star charcoal. Bon apetit.”

Jordan snorts, gives in, and takes a bite. It’s actually not that bad. The jam does do a heroic work at hiding all the burnt parts.”

“Not awful.”

“High praise, coming from you,” John says. “There’s juice. And also, I got you something.”

John hesitates, then reaches under his pillow and pulls out a little red envelope. It’s fat, corners slightly bent from clearly being hidden badly. 

“Johnny, you didn’t have to…”

“I know I don’t,” John interrupts. “I wanted to.”

He shoves it at Jordan, then immediately curls in on himself, arms around his knees, watching with an anxious curiosity.

“Open it, then.”

Jordan wipes his glittery fingers on the duvet (which he will complain about later, then blame John for) and tears the envelop open. A folded card spills out, hand-drawn hearts all over the front. Something heavier thuds onto the sheets. He picks that up first.

It’s a keyring. A tiny acrylic version of the two of them, some fanart – John must’ve tracked the artist down. They’re both in an England kit, John with a tongue between his teeth and Jordan behind him, arms around his waist, kissing the top of his head. Little pink hearts drawn around them. 

“Christ,” he breathes. “You’re such a sap.”

John groans, throwing himself onto his back and covering his face with both hands. “I’m disgusting. I deserve to be locked away.”

Jordan laughs, and flicks the card open. Inside, John’s handwriting is all over the place, lines sloping down because for some reason that boy can’t write in a straight line.

Pickers,

I know we said no big gifts this year because we’re trying to be grown-ups or whatever it is you said. But you’re stuck with me, so here we are.

Thank you for making everywhere feel like home, even when we’re in shitty hotels with thin walls. Thank you for shouting at me when I leave my socks everywhere and then kissing me anyway. I know you can’t stand to be mad at me.

You’re my favourite person in this world.

Happy Valentine’s Day, you big softie. I love you.

John x

P.S. The keyring’s so you will always come home to me

Jordan stares at the words until they blur a little. His throat’s properly closed up now. He swallows the lump, card trembling between his fingers, and drags his gaze up. John’s peaking at him through his fingers, eyes wide with that bare-faced hope that still floors him after all this time. 

“Well?” John says. “It’s not too cringe, is it? I can write you a new one that just says u up? If you want. Granted it won’t be–”

Jordan tosses the card onto the bedside table and launches himself forward, flattening John into the mattress. He lets out a delighted oof from beneath him, arms immediately winding around.

“I love you,” Jordan mutters into his neck, then pulls back enough to kiss him. John tastes like jam and burnt bread. He slots a hand under the back of his shirt, and feels John shiver. 

When they finally break apart, John’s eyes are soft and shiny. “So… you like it?”

“‘Course I do,” Jordan says. “I’m keepin’ that card forever. Gonna laminate it. Put it up at Finch, maybe, so everyone has to read how much you fancy me.”

That earns him a laugh. Jordan drags his mouth down to the line of his jaw and murmurs, “come here, baby. Get those pretty lips on me.”

John’s eyes flutter like he’s just been handed the best present of his life. “N-now?”

“Now,” Jordan says. “All I can think about ever since I woke up covered in bloody glitter.”

John’s already shifting, already sliding down the bed. His palms skim over Jordan’s ribs, his hips, his thighs. There’s a moment where he looks up, kneeling between Jordan’s legs. Something soft flickers across his face, this quiet, adoring thing.

“Fuck,” he mumbles, leaning in. “Missed him.”

He thinks he’s overdone it. John blinks at him, eyes shining. His grip on the jar tightens. “Oh.”

John nods. “Yeah,” he manages. “Okay. Fuck, Jord, this is–” he breaks off, laughing helplessly. “You wrote me stuff. For everyday.”

“Five?” Jordan repeats, scandalised. “You can’t open five! That’s cheating.”

“Please,” John says, scooting closer, clutching the jar to his chest. “Come on, Jords. You can’t expect me to wait. Just five.”

“That’s not how this works, John.”

That last bit tickles something in him. He takes a look at him, at the dark smudges under his eyes from restless nights, the faint tension still sitting in his shoulders even now. How he’s clinging to the jar like it’s a life jacket.

He sighs. “Five. And that’s it. You try and sneak in a sixth, I’m takin’ it off and putting a padlock on the lid.”

Jordan’s not sure John even heard him. He opens it very gently, fingers slipping inside to rustle among the little papers until he pinches one out at random. He unfolds it slowly, skimming the handwriting. His mouth curves, whole face lighting up.

“What’s that one say?” Jordan asks. 

“I love you because you always pretend not to like being looked after, even though you melt the second I bring you a cup of tea.” John’s cheeks flush. “That’s not true.”

“It’s extremely true.”

John smiles anyway, folding the note carefully and setting it on the bedside table. “Next one.”

He picks another.

“I love you because you talk to dogs like they’re people and to people like they’re dogs,” John snorts, laughing properly now. “Okay, that’s fair.”

“Go on.”

Third.

John’s smile goes soft at that one. He presses his lips together, looking down at the little scrap in his hand.

“Fourth,” he digs around more carefully this time. Then pulls one out and opens it. “I love you because you fought. Even when it would’ve been easier to give up.”

Jordan watches John. He remembers ugly arguments and slammed doors and weeks of silence that tasted like ash, and the way John still turned up despite everything, stubborn and furious, and said, I don’t want to be anywhere else.

“Last one,” John whispers. “I love you because you never realise how brave you are, and you chose me to remind you every day.”

The tears are back. He doesn’t bother pretending this time. They sit in his lashes, making his blue eyes shine in the fairy-light glow. “Jordan…”

Jordan reaches out, takes the little note from his fingers and adds it to the tiny pile on the bedside table. Then he cups John’s jaw, sweeping away the tear that finally slips free.

“Five,” he says gently. “That’s your lot.”

John nods, sniffling, then suddenly throws himself forward, knocking the jar lid back on with one hand and pressing his face into Jordan’s neck with the other. His voice is muffled when he speaks, choked and overwhelmed all at once. “I love you. I love you so much.”

Jordan wraps both arms around him, the jar in between their hearts. He kisses his hair, his cheeks, his damp eyelashes again for good measure. “Love you too, Johnny. More each day.”

John lets out a shaky little laugh, relaxing fully against him at last. His body fits into Jordan’s like it’s learned the shape by heart.

“You’re gonna regret giving me these, you know.”

“Why’s that?”

“Because,” John mumbles, “every time I forget you’ll have to sit through me reading them out. Over and over again.”

Jordan gives him a quiet laugh. “I think I can live with that.”

He presses his lips to the crown of John’s head, breathing him in, and lets his eyes fall shut.

“Happy Valentine’s, pet,” he whispers once more.

title: so it goes
summary: Rúben plans their Valentine’s date, but then all of his cleverly thought out plans fail, and it looks like this is going to go down in history as the worst Valentine’s Day ever. John “punishes” him in his own way.
body:
The roses start dying at midday. Well – they’re not actually dying, but that’s what it looks like to Rúben when he walks back into the kitchen and finds his perfect, deep-red bouquet listing in the vase. One of the stem has slipped, the head drooping over the rim, petals pressed against the glass.

“Great,” he mutters. “Of course today.”

He’d trimmed them at an angle, used the little packet of flower food like the woman in the shop had told him. He rearranged them twice until they looked perfect. And now they’re collapsing. 

The timer on the oven beeps then. He goes over and pulls the door open. When he does, the lasagna is… not golden. In fact, it’s black.

“Fuck off,” he says, staring at it. “No. No, no, no.”

He doesn’t understand how this could’ve happened. He followed the recipe exactly to the doot, measured everything. He even checked halfway through. And now there’s only this sad, burnt rectangle that smells like the inside of a chimney.

The plan, in his head, is simple. It has been simple for weeks.

Breakfast in bed, with flowers. Then the museum John’s been on about but never had time for. Then an early dinner at the little place on the corner that agreed to close just for them. Walk by the river after, fairy lights and all, then home for dessert and yeah. Perfect. Thoughtful. Romantic, without being sickly.

But here he is, with dead roses and lasagna burnt to shit.

His phone buzzes on the counter. He wipes his hands on a towel and picks it up.

J 🩵: Leaving now!! Be home in 15. Love you rubes x

He stares at the little blue heart until he breathes a bit. Okay. It’s one burnt meal, everything else can still go right. 

He shoves the lasagna tin onto the stovetop, opens the fridge and eyes the emergency option he’d hidden at the back. A high-end ready meal. He’d wanted to do it all himself this time, homemade from scratch, but life has other plans it seems. He rips the packaging open, chucks it in the oven, sets a new timer and then goes back to the flowers, turning them so the best sides face out. Just in time, too, because he hears the front door open a moment later.

“Love?” John calls.

“In here,” he answers, trying not to sound like a man who has just murdered lasagna.

John appears in the doorway, grocery bag in one hand. He grins when he sees the roses, eyes going wide. 

“Woah,” he lights up, stepping closer. “Look at you. House smells nice.”

It smells like burnt cheese, but sure.

Rúben leans in to kiss him. “Happy Valentine’s.”

“You too,” John noses at his cheek for a second, then pulls back to inspect the bouquet, grazing a velvety petal. “These are gorgeous. You didn’t have to do all this, y’know.”

“I wanted to,” he says. “This is just the beginning.”
